summary:
Post-operative nausea and vomiting commonly result from factors such as blood absorption during surgery, use of nitrous oxide anesthesia, opioid administration; however, acetaminophen typically only causes gastrointestinal issues at high doses unrelated to typical surgical recovery scenarios. Thus,
The exception here is D) Acetaminophen since its usual dosage levels do not directly induce post-op nausea without additional complications. To clarify:

Post-operative nausea and vomiting often stem from intra-surgical conditions including bleeding, anesthetic agents used, and pain management with opioids but rarely due solely to standard usage of acetaminophen which generally affects metabolism rather than causing direct GI distress under normal medical protocols. So
Acetaminophen should stand out uniquely amongst listed options regarding inducing PONV [post- operative nausea &呕吐]. 

Key Points Condensed into Two Sentences:
Nausea and vomiting after operations usually arise from specific peri-procedural events involving blood loss, certain gases/medications administered intravenously/anesthetically, along with opiate-based analgesics – whereas common over-the-counter medications like acetaminophen don’t independently trigger these side effects when prescribed appropriately within recommended limits pre/post surgically. 
Thus excluding A,B,C leaves us concluding that D-acetaminophen correctly identifies what wouldn't normally lead to significant PO(N)V episodes outside unusual overdose situations impacting organ function beyond mere digestive irritation.
